coredata.dc:title: Biomass converted carbon quantum dots for all-weather solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yuanyuan
Zhang, Yue
Sun, Weiyin
Wang, Min
He, Benlin
Chen, Haiyan
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: A great challenge for state-of-the-art photovoltaic devices is to realize electric power generation in all weathers. We constructively demonstrate here the conversion from biomass to carbon quantum dots for all-weather carbon quantum dot solar cells that can generate electricity in the daytime and in the dark. The combination of green-emitting long persistence phosphors with mesoscopic titanium dioxide realizes optical storage by composite photoanode under illumination and excitation to monochromatic green light in the dark. The optimized all-weather solar cell yields maximized dark power conversion efficiency as high as 7.97% along with persistent electricity output for several hours. This work begins a photovoltaic revolution to forward all-weather solar cells as future energy solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-weather solar cell
Dye-sensitized solar cell
Energy harvest
Photoanode
Photovoltaic device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Conversion from soybean to CQDs. (b) Synthesis strategies of CQDs from soybean starch. The schematic diagrams of all-weather CQD solar cell (c) under sunlight irradiation and (d) at dark-light environments. (e) The photoluminescence image of CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanode after irradiation by a simulated sunlight (AM1.5, 100mWcm−2) for 5min.

(a) Images for CQD aqueous solutions synthesized at different heating times. (b) Images of CQD aqueous solutions synthesized at different heating times under UV light irradiation. (c) FTIR spectrum of CQDs synthesized at a heating time of 16h. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs synthesized at a heating time of 16h in diluted solutions. (e) PL emission spectra of the CQDs synthesized at a heating time of 16h under different excitation wavelengths. (f) Raman spectrum of CQDs at a heating time of 16h. (e) XPS of CQDs at a heating time of 16h. (g) EDS spectra of CQDs at heating times of 16h.

(a) Optical transmission of various electrodes. (b) The photo and dark J–V characteristics of traditional solar cell without LPPs. (c) Characteristic photo and (d) dark J–V curves for all-weather CQD solar cells. The dark J–V curves are recorded in atmosphere with no light illumination. All photo J–V curves are measured under simulated sunlight (AM1.5, 100mWcm−2).

(a) CV curve of Pt foil supported CQDs recorded in 0.1M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile. The heating time for CQD was 16h. (b) The energy-level diagram and charge-transfer processes.

(a) PL emission spectra of the m-TiO2/LPP photoanodes sensitized by CQDs at a heating time of 16h. The PL spectra are recorded at different excitation wavelengths. (b) The Nyquist EIS plots for corresponding solar cells. The insert shows an equivalent circuit for charge-transfer processes.

(a) Fluorescence decay of CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanode taken at different afterglow times after irradiation by a simulated solar light at a light intensity of 100mWcm−2 for 5min. (b) Afterglow intensity monitored by a standard silicon solar cell as a function of time. The evolutions of photovoltaic parameters versus decay time: (c) dark V oc, (d) dark J sc and (e) dark FF.
The corresponding photovoltaic parameters extracted from CQD solar cells with or without green-emitting LPPs.
The corresponding photovoltaic parameters extracted from all-weather CQD solar cells at different decay times. The CQDs are synthesized at a heating time of 16h.

head.ce:title: Biomass converted carbon quantum dots for all-weather solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A great challenge for state-of-the-art photovoltaic devices is to realize electric power generation in all weathers. We constructively demonstrate here the conversion from biomass to carbon quantum dots for all-weather carbon quantum dot solar cells that can generate electricity in the daytime and in the dark. The combination of green-emitting long persistence phosphors with mesoscopic titanium dioxide realizes optical storage by composite photoanode under illumination and excitation to monochromatic green light in the dark. The optimized all-weather solar cell yields maximized dark power conversion efficiency as high as 7.97% along with persistent electricity output for several hours. This work begins a photovoltaic revolution to forward all-weather solar cells as future energy solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy utilization has been a significant strategy for renewable energy production. By building solar cell devices [1–3], the solar radiation can be converted into electricity according to complicated photochemical reactions. Till now, the power conversion efficiencies of all kinds of solar cells have experienced persistent rises through breakthroughs in key material innovation, structure reorganization, and technology optimization [4]. However, it is still a great challenge for state-of-the-art solar cells to generate electricity under sun irradiation at sunny days as well as in cloudy, rainy, foggy and night etc dark-light conditions. A common bottleneck problem is the low light irradiation in these dark environments, leading to incapable light-absorber excitation for electricity output.

Recently, we have successfully made sun and rain triggered all-weather solar cells by incorporating reduced graphene oxide (rGO) film with traditional flexible solar cell devices by a hot-press process [5], yielding a conversion efficiency of 6.53% under simulated full-spectral sunlight (air mass 1.5, 100mWcm−2) illumination as well as current of microamps and voltage over hundreds of microvolts by simulated rain. The photoelectrochemical processes under sunlight obey photoelectric mechanisms, while electricity production under rain arises from charging/discharging cycles of deionized π-electron (rGO)|cation (rain) electric double layer pseudocapacitances [5,6]. Guided by this theory, electrophoric deposited graphene [7] and graphene tailored conducting coatings [8] have also been applied to such all-weather solar cells for enhanced energy harvest from sun and rain. Previous works on this field open an era of developing multifunctional solar cells to explore energy harvesting beyond sun illumination. However, there are still some major limitations for such architectures: (i) The rain enabled solar cells have low electricity outputs under rain droplets; (ii) The reversal at rainy days introduces tedious procedures; (iii) The efficiencies of these solar cells are still zero at nights and relatively low at cloudy and foggy days. One solution to this impasse is to invent all-weather solar cells that are applicable in all weathers.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new family members as light absorbers in photovoltaics, arising from their peculiar performances in photon absorption and electron excitation (maximal extinction coefficient of 105 M−1 cm−1, about an order of magnitude higher than that of commonly used metal complexes) [9] as well as tunable energy levels. Growing attentions have been paid to build CQD solar cells as cost-effective energy harvesters [10–26]. Long persistence phosphors (LPPs) are optical materials that can emit fluorescent light for hours after ceasing excitation by wide-spectral sunlight [13,27], allowing them for versatile applications as night or dark-light vision materials in many technological fields [14,28]. Stimulated by the extraordinary performances, we present here the establishment of all-weather solar cell architectures by combining green-emitting LPP phosphors with mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) for storing optical energy from unabsorbed sunlight and gradually releasing monochromatic green light in dark-light weathers. The green luminescence emitted by m-TiO2/LPP photoanode persistently illuminates CQDs for electron excitation, allowing solar cells for producing electricity in all weathers.

Soybean powders were purchased from Jiangsu JingMoFang Co., Ltd. Ultrafine green-emitting LPP phosphors were purchased from Shenzhen HuiDuoSheng Luminous Material Co., Ltd. Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate, tetramethylammonium iodide, tetrabutylammonium iodide, 4-tert-butyl-pyridine and tetraethylammonium iodide were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Acetonitrile, NaI, KI, LiI and I2 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all the experiments was purified through redistillation process.

CQDs were prepared according to the following procedures using soybean powders as raw materials. Under vigorous agitation, 0.5g of ultrafine soybean powders was dissolved in 40mL of deionized water. After 10min, the reagent solution was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The reaction was kept at 170°C for 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 and 30h to obtain CQD aqueous solutions. Subsequently, the CQDs were stored at 5°C for use. The energy levels of CQD were measured by a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method using a standard three-electrode system consisting of a working electrode of platinum foil supported CQDs, a counter electrode of platinum wire, a reference electrode of Ag/AgCl, and a supporting electrolyte of 0.1M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile. The CV curve was scanned from −1.5 to 1.5V at a scanning rate of 50mVs−1.

Colloidal TiO2 films with thickness of around 10μm and active area of 0.5×0.5cm2 was fabricated by coating TiO2 colloid onto freshly cleaned FTO glass substrates (12Ω square−1) with size of 2×2cm2 by a doctor-blade method [29]. Subsequently, ultrafine green-emitting LPP phosphors were dispersed in anhydrous ethanol for a 0.25gmL−1 solution, which was spin-coated onto TiO2 layer at a rotation speed of 2000rpm for 20s. Finally, the FTO glass supported TiO2/LPP films were calcined in a muffle furnace at 450°C for 30min in air. The resultant m-TiO2/LPP photoanodes were immersed in concentrated CQD aqueous solutions for 12h to obtain CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanodes. The model number for green-emitting LPP phosphors was H–1B in the selling company. As references, CQD sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes were also prepared according to the same procedures without incorporating LPP phosphors.

The pyrolyzed platinum electrode on FTO glass was used as a counter electrode. Each solar cell device was built by sandwiching a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte between a platinum counter electrode and a CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP (or m-TiO2) photoanode and sealed with a Surlyn film (30μm in thickness). A liquid electrolyte consisting of 0.1M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1M tetramethylammonium iodide, 0.1M tetrabutylammonium iodide, 0.1M NaI, 0.1M KI, 0.1M LiI, 0.05M I2 and 0.05M 4-tert-butyl-pyridine in acetonitrile.

The photo photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of all-weather solar cells with an active area of 0.25cm2 were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation under irradiation of simulated solar light from a 100W Xenon arc lamp in ambient atmosphere. The incident light intensity was controlled to 100mWcm−2 (calibrated by a standard silicon solar cell). A black mask was applied on the surface of cell device to avoid stray light. Each J-V curve was repeated measured at least 30 times using different samples to eliminate experimental errors. Before measurement for dark J-V curves, the solar cells were illuminated by a simulated solar light for 1min. Immediately, the devices were covered in a completely dark condition and the dark J-V curves were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in air. The fluorescent light intensities of FTO glass supported m-TiO2/LPP films were also recorded by a standard silicon solar cell. Therefore, the dark cell efficiency was obtained according to η = P max/P in, where P max and P in were the maximal power output and fluorescent intensity of m-TiO2/LPP anode, respectively.

Using soybean powders as raw materials, CQDs can be facilely synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the reaction temperature is controlled at 170°C, and reaction time is tuned from 4 to 30h to maximize photoelectric performances. The SAED pattern reveals a polycrystalline structure of biomass converted CQDs, while surface roughness demonstrates one carbon layer for these CQDs. The mechanism behind this conversion is that starch molecules from soybean can hydrolyze into glucose (Fig. 1b), which can subsequently convert into CQDs through complicated dehydration processes [30]. The primary elements are 53.73 at% C and 46.27 at% O for the CQDs at a heating time of 16h (Fig. 2 h), indicating existence of oxygenic groups such as hydroxyls, carboxyls, or carbonyls which also been detected at heating time of 4h and 30h in Fig. S1. This deduction can be confirmed by the detection of OH, CH, CO, and C-O groups in its FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2c). Detection of CC bonds means honeycomb-like structure of CQD, while COC group at 1404cm−1 suggests oxygen is doped into its planar structure. As has been previously demonstrated that the formation of CQDs obeys nucleation and epitaxial growth [31], thus CQD size and therefore photosensitiveness are dependent on hydrothermal time. Fig. 2a shows the images of CQD solutions as a function of heating time from 4 to 30h. Apparently, the color darkens with increase of heating time, suggesting an elevated CQD concentration. From the CQD solutions irradiated by the ultraviolet lamp, as shown in Fig. 2b, there is no obvious change in emission color but the solution at a heating time of 16h is brightest, referring to a maximized photoexcitation behavior. The d spacing between graphene layers is determined to be 0.33nm (Fig. 1a), equaling to basal plane distance of bulk graphite [32].

Fig. 2d stacks the UV–vis absorption spectra of corresponding CQDs at diluted aqueous solutions, showing reduced absorbances but same absorption peak positions with increase of dilution fold. Two characteristic peaks at 228 and 278nm are related to π→π* of CC [33], and n → π* transition of CO bond [34], respectively. The linear relationship between absorbance and dilution times for 278nm suggests the photoabsorption of corresponding CQDs is independent on their concentrations. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs solution, shown in Fig. 2e, demonstrate a broad emission band centered at 500nm when suffering excitation by 390nm. The PL peak shifts from 500 to 579nm by changing excitation wavelength from 410 to 550nm. A maximized PL intensity is determined at an excitation wavelength of 470nm, and the quantum yield is estimated to be 7.14%. This arises from the decoration of functional oxygenic groups (-OH, CO, CO, COC, etc), forming surface states energy levels between π and π* states of CC segments [20,34]. When suffering excitation under different wavelength, the emission traps from different oxygenic groups are also changed to emit tunable emission bands [35,36]. Raman spectra are performed to study the CQD structure. The determination of D band at 1326cm−1 and G band at 1576cm−1 suggests the characteristic Raman peaks of carbon atom crystal. The D band, arising from imperfection or destruction of sp2 hybridization, is associated with disordered induce-scattering (Fig. 2f), which generally ascribed to the conjugated structure destruction by oxygenic groups and small sp2 cluster size. The G band generally corresponds to E 2g mode of graphite and vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. By comparing ID/IG of the CQDs (ID/IG =0.68) with 0.1 for graphite, one can confirm that the hydrothermal method reported here is efficient for CQD synthesis. According to typical fittings for graphite oxide, chemical bond of C1s XPS spectrum about CQDs (Fig. 2g) can be deconvoluted into three carbon moieties: CC (284.4eV), C-O (286.0eV), CO (288.0eV). Because of the existence of those oxygenic groups, CQDs can tightly integrate with the functional group which on the surface of m-TiO2, which can be provided by EDS element mapping data of C elements throughout the TiO2/CQD composites in Fig. S2.

Moreover, Fig. 1c represents sandwiched architecture of an all-weather CQD solar cell including a CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanode, a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte, and a platinum counter electrode. When irradiated by sunlight from photoanode, the incident light with wavelength >372nm is not completely absorbed by CQDs (Fig. 3 a), and this unabsorbed light can be stored by green-emitting LPP phosphors for persistent green luminescence emission, allowing for CQD irradiation at dark-light conditions (Fig. 1d and e). The reduced tranmission suggests ∼20% of incident light including red and infrared light has been reutilized by CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanode.

As shown in Fig. 3b, the solar cell without green-emitting LPP phosphors yields a short-circuit current density (J sc) of 259μAcm−2 and open-circuit voltage (V oc) of 0.508V under AM1.5 (100mWcm−2) illumination. Although the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are lower than state-of-the-art photovoltaic devices, they are still larger than previous reports [37,38]. The main reason may be attributed to the re-absorption of non-utilized incident light beyond 600nm by green-emitting LPP phosphors and subsequently emitting the green-light which can excite the CQDs to enhance the current density, in other words, expanding the photo-response range of solar spectrum.

By recording CV curve in a three-electrode system, as shown in Fig. 4 a, typical oxidation/reduction peaks are detected. The LUMO and HOMO levels of CQDs are calculated to be −3.85 and −5.28eV according to E LUMO =−e(E red +4.4)V, E HOMO =−e(E ox +4.4)V, respectively. Therefore, the CQDs can absorb photons to release electrons, flowing from LUMO (-3.85eV) to conduction band of TiO2 (−4.2eV). The holes jump from HOMO level (-5.28eV) to redox potential (-4.90eV) of I−/I3 − couples, as shown in Fig. 4b. The dark J-V curve passes coordinate origin, suggesting that both current and voltage outputs are zero [39–43]. When incorporated by green-emitting LPP phosphors, the corresponding all-weather solar cells have similar J-V curves and comparable photovoltaic parameters in comparison to state-of-the-art solar cell device (Fig. 3c and Table 1 ), and the solar cell with the CQDs at a heating time of 16h has maximized power conversion efficiency (η). Interestingly, the dark J sc is 30μAcm−2, V oc is 0.347V, and fill factor (FF) is 66.1% without simulated sunlight illumination, generating an optimal η of 7.97%, as shown in Fig. 3d. The corresponding characterizations demonstrate that raised solar cells are capable of persistently generating electricity under sunlight irradiation and dark environments. A potential mechanism behind this high dark η value for all-weather CQD solar cell is attributed to monochromatic greent-fluorescence emission for CQD illumination. As has been mentioned previously that the green-emitting LPP phosphors can store energy from unabsorbed light with λ>372nm and emit green luminescence to persistently illuminate CQDs for electron photogeneration. The PL spectra of CQDs in Fig. 2e suggest characteristic light-response of CQDs toward green light with wavelength of 500∼560nm. Moreover, a well known superiority is the high absorption and conversion of solar cells toward emitted green light. From good repeatability shown in Fig. S3-S8, we can infer that all-weather solar cells using bean converted CQDs can be realized using the method reported here. This conclusion can also be supported by the relatively high effficiency (19.5%) of dye-sensitized solar cells under monochromatic fluorescent LED irradiation [44].

The PL emission spectra of CQDs sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanodes are performed to cross-check the sensitivity of CQDs at dark conditions. The corresponding CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanodes have broad emission peaks centered at around 510nm, indicating green-light emission. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the emission peak starts at 467nm and end at 577nm under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 450 to 900nm. Combined with PL emission spectra in Fig. 2e, all the fluorescent light having wavelength of 467∼577nm can excite CQDs to create electrons. Therefore, we can make two conclusions according to these characterizations: (i) Both down-converted and up-converted light can be absorbed by green-emitting LPP phosphors for electricity generation at dark conditions; (ii) The raised all-weather CQD solar cells have markedly widened the light-absorption range.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is performed to study the charge-transfer processes within solar cells. The corresponding plots are stacked in Fig. 5b and electrochemical parameters are summarized in Table S1 by fitting EIS plots with an equivalent circuit. The charge-transfer resistance at photoanode/electrolyte interface (R ct2) has a valley for m-TiO2/LPP anode sensitized with CQDs at a heating time of 16h. This is mainly attributed to facile photoexcitation of CQDs as well as accelerated photoelectrochemical reaction kinetics under increased photogenerated electron density.

Apart from dark efficiency, the long-term stability [45,46] is also crucial to promote real application of such all-weather solar cells. Fig. 6 a shows the fluorescence decay of m-TiO2/LPP photoanode sensitized with CQDs at a heating time of 16h. The internal rectangle is filled with CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP, while external one is only covered by green-emitting LPP phosphors. At the same decay time, the CQD sensitized m-TiO2/LPP is darker than LPP-only layer due to the light absorption of CQDs. The lighteness reduces with fluorescence decay, referring to gradual release of stored light energy. The photoanode is still visible at a decay time of 4h. By plotting light intensity versus decay time, as shown in Fig. 6b, the initial fluorescence intensity is 67μWcm−2 after irradiation by a simulated solar light at a light intensity of 100mWcm−2 for 5min, and it abruptly to <10μWcm−2 at a decay time of 30s. Subsequently, the light intensity remains nearly unchanged for several hours, and it is 0.03μWcm−2 after lasting for 4h. Arising from persistent illumination of green aftergrow, the all-weather CQD solar cells allow for creating electricity for a long time at dark conditions (Fig. S9). The plots of photovoltaic parameters including dark J sc, V oc and FF as a function of decay time are shown in Fig. 6c-e and the data are summarized in Table 2 . Abrupt reductions are also detected for photovoltaic parameters in first 10min, the initial J sc and V oc values reduce to 8.71μAcm−2 and 0.241V from 43.8μAcm−2 and 0.36V, respectively. Application requirements for future all-weather solar cell fabrication should be focused on developing robust LPP phosphors with long-time emission at high fluorescence intensity. The current work has realized the high-efficiency light absorption and persistent power generation in dark-light conditions, but there are still some limitations. For example, the photo efficiency of CQD tailored device is still low in comparison with state-of-the-art mesoscopic solar cells because of limited light emission. A solution is to co-sensitize mesoscopic TiO2 with commercial dyes. Another problem for all-weather solar cells is that the power outputs in dark-light conditions is still low because of the weak photofluorescence of LPP phosphors. By increasing LPP performances such as photofluorescent intensity and lifetime, the photovoltaic properties of so-called all-weather solar cells may be markedly enhanced.

In summary, the successful conversion from biomass to CQDs through a hydrothermal method has been constructively demonstrated. All-weather CQD solar cells that can generate electricity in the daytime and in the dark have been built by using the featured photoelectron excitation behaviors of CQDs and light storing/emissing performances of LPPs. A maximized photoelectric conversion efficiency as high as 7.97% along with persistent electricity output for several hours has been achieved in completely dark conditions. The results and concepts of all-weather solar cells guide us to explore advanced photovoltaic device for persistent electric power generation in any weather.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202, 61604143, 61774139), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (JQ201714) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (201564002, 201762018)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.10.086.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
